---
title: Ex.1 Parsing Protein Structures
format: html
Authors: "Marta Aransay, Carlota de la Concepción, Marta Rodrigo, Lucía Santos"
date: 04/03/2026
---

# Introduction

Structural bioinformatics integrates computational methods, data resources and algorithms to study, analyze and predict the three-dimensional organization of biomacromolecules. High-resolution structural information  provides insights into biological function and enables the characterization of molecular effects of mutations, ligand binding and intermolecular interactions.

Proteins are macromolecules whose function is highly related to their three-dimensional structure. Their organization can be described in four levels, which depend on folding. Protein folding is driven by non-covalent interactions between atoms of residues of the same molecule, intramolecular, or other molecules, intermolecular. These interactions include hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions and electrostatic forces. Structural bioinformatics aims to characterize these interactions through computational analysis of experimentally determined or predicted structures.

The main repository of experimentally determined macromolecuar structures is the Protein Data Bank (PDB), which stores three-dimensional structures obtained by X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and cryo-electron microscopy. PDB files contain detailed metadata, experimental information, protein sequences, and three-dimensional atomic coordinates. Through computational analysis, this information can be extracted from these files.

To analyze protein architecture contact maps are commonly used as a simplified representation of the three-dimensional structure. These maps use pairwise distance matrices to provide a two-dimensional visualization of the interactions between the C$\alpha$  of all the residues in a protein. A distance threshold is established to determine if two residues interact or not. Specific patterns are associated with specific secondary structure elements, so it is possible to infer the spatial organization of proteins.

Nowadays, although modelling and structure prediction methods have achieved high levels of accuracy, the analysis of atomic coordinates is still essential to understand the spatial organization of proteins and macromolecular complexes.


## Objective

The main objective of this project is to implement computational functions to:

1. Parse PDB files to extract structural metadata and atomic coordinates.
2. Compute pairwise distance matrices between C$\alpha$ atoms of protein residues.
3. Plot contact maps to characterize the structural organization and oligomeric state of the analyzed proteins.

---

# Methods

## Data Collection

RCSB-PDB (Research Collaboratory for Structural Bioinformatics Protein Data Bank, https://www.rcsb.org/) is a repository of experimentally determined, and also computationally predicted, structures of proteins, nucleic acids and macromolecular complexes. 

Each protein in this database is identified with a unique four-character alphanumeric identifier. This identifier allows access to detailed information about the protein, such as atomic coordinates, sequence, experimental methodology and associated metadata.

The structures analyzed in this project correspond to the following proteins:

- **Plasma Retinol-Binding Protein Precursor (*Homo sapiens*, PDB ID: *1RBP*)**, which is involved in retinol transport in humans.
- **Carbonic Anhydrase (*Methanosarcina thermophila*, PDB ID: *1QRE*)**, which regulates pH by catalyzing the reaction between $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ to produce bicarbonate and protons.
- **Catalase (*Homo sapiens*, PDB ID: *1DGF*)**, which cells from oxidative damage by decomposing $H_2O_2$ into water and oxygen.

## Parsing PDB Files

The data base PDB is one of the most widely used worldwide in the field of bioinformatics, among other aspects for its accessibility and the large amount of information it contains. Taking advantage of the possibility of querying through its API, we have been able to extract the necessary data through a parsing function @parse_pdb_function.

The objective of this function is to parse the PDB files of the studied proteins, extracting the relevant information for subsequent analysis. Specifically, we have obtained the descriptive and experimental information associated with each entry of the Protein Data Bank.

The function defined as @parse_pdb_function performs two independent queries to the RCSB PDB REST API (https://data.rcsb.org/redoc/index.html), one at the structural level (https://data.rcsb.org/redoc/index.html#tag/Entry-Service/operation/getEntryById) and another at the polymer entity level (https://data.rcsb.org/redoc/index.html#tag/Entity-Service/operation/getPolymerEntityById). This separation allows us to access both the experimental and general details of the protein and the specific information related to it. 

In first place, the function implements a basic system of error handling to ensure that the queries are successful. It handles errors due to timeouts and inexistent entries (HTTP status code 404).

Once the queries are successful, the function extracts the relevant information from the JSON responses:

```{python}
import pandas as pd

table_info = pd.DataFrame({
    "Metadata": ["PDB ID", "Protein Name", "Source Organism", "Determination Method", "Authors", "Release Date", "Resolution", "Number of Peptide Chains", "Number of Other Molecules"],
    "Description": ["Unique identifier for the protein structure", "The name of the protein", "The organism from which the protein was derived", "The method used to determine the protein structure (e.g., X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy)", "The researchers who contributed to the determination of the structure", "The release date of the structure", "The resolution of the structure, if applicable", "The number of peptide chains present in the structure", "The number of other molecules (e.g., ligands, water) present in the structure"],
    "Source (API JSON field)": ["rcsb_id", "rcsb_polymer_entity.pdbx_description", "rcsb_entity_source_organism", "exptl.method", "rcsb_authors", "rcsb_accession_info.initial_release_date", "rcsb_entry_info.resolution_combined", "rcsb_entry_info.polymer_entity_count_protein", "rcsb_entry_info.nonpolymer_entity_count"]
})

```

There are also some fields with multiple values, such as authors, experimental methods, etc. For those cases, an auxiliary function @extract_metadata_function is used to concatenate the elements of the list into a single string, separating the values with commas. This facilitates the presentation of the information and its subsequent analysis.

All this information is organized into a structured fromat, as it is a dictionary, which allows its conversion into a pandas DataFrame and its export to a CSV file for further analysis and visualization. 

In case of any error during the queries, None is returned. 



## Metadata Extraction

## Distance and Contact Map Calculations

[Primero hacer una introduccion a que es la distancia el mapa de contacto (por qué son útiles), meter alguna explicacion matemática, y luego lo mismo que antes, describir las partes más importantes del código.]

\[
d_{ij}^2 = (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 + (z_i - z_j)^2
\]

---

# Results

TABLA CON LOS RESULTADOS DE LAS DISTANCIAS Y CONTACTOS

---

# Discussion

---

# Conclusion

---

# Annexes

## Work Register

[Aquí básicamente pondría el paso a paso que hemos seguido en github, con los commits, etc. para que se vea el proceso de trabajo]

The development of this project has followed a workflow managed via GitHub, in order it can be reproduced.

The following table sumarizes the main stages of the project.

```{python}
#| echo: false
table_github = pd.DataFrame({
    "Phase": [
        "Setup", 
        "Metadata", 
        "Protein structural information", 
        "Distance matrix", 
        "Contact maps", 
        "Report writing"], 
    "Description":[
        "Repository initialization and project structuring", 
        "Access to each protein key information via APIs and store it in a dataframe", 
        "Get access to PDB files and parse them to gen atomic coordinates",
        "Compute the distances matrix between $C_{\\alpha}$ atoms",
        "Plot intra/interchain contacts giving a threshold",
        "Create the final file and include all the previous analysis"]})
table_github
```

Every modification is documented through a descriptive commit message.

**Repository Access**: https://github.com/MCML-Projects/STRBIO.git
